past_medical_history: Mild obstructive sleep apnea, non-compliant with CPAP
Remote history of palpitations evaluated 2 years ago with unremarkable Holter monitor at that time
family_history: Father died suddenly at age 48 while playing basketball, cause listed as cardiac arrest on death certificate
Paternal uncle died in his sleep at age 42, no autopsy performed
Mother alive at age 78, has hypertension and type 2 diabetes
No known family history of epilepsy or neurological disease

social_history.drugs: Denies illicit drug use, no history of stimulant use
social_history.occupation: High school physical education teacher and assistant football coach
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife and two teenage children in a suburban home
social_history.exercise: Very active, jogs 3-4 times per week, coaches football practice daily during season

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, fully oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech is fluent with normal rate and prosody, no dysarthria or aphasia. Attention intact, serial 7s performed without error. Registration 3/3, recall 3/3 at 5 minutes. No confusion or disorientation noted. Patient is cooperative and provides a clear, coherent history.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive 3mm to 2mm. Fundoscopic exam unremarkable, no papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus, no diplopia. CN V: Sensation intact in all divisions bilaterally, masseter strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation, no facial droop. CN VIII: Hearing grossly intact to finger rub bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, uvula midline. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue protrudes midline, no atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk and tone normal throughout all extremities. No rigidity or spasticity. Strength 5/5 in all muscle groups tested (deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, finger extensors, grip, interossei, hip flexors, knee extensors, knee flexors, ankle dorsiflexors, ankle plantarflexors) bilaterally. No pronator drift. No tremor.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception intact throughout all four extremities. No sensory level. No dermatomal deficits.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing normal bilaterally without dysmetria or intention tremor. Heel-to-shin smooth bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact and symmetric.
neurological_exam.gait: Normal stride length and base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative. No ataxia or unsteadiness.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue laceration. No evidence of urinary incontinence. Small abrasion on left elbow from fall during most recent episode. No bruising patterns to suggest prolonged convulsive activity.

patient.chief_complaint: Two episodes of loss of consciousness with shaking, referred as new-onset seizures
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Darnell Hawkins is a 55-year-old physically active man who presents to the emergency department after being referred by his primary care physician for evaluation of two episodes of loss of consciousness with witnessed 'shaking,' provisionally diagnosed as new-onset seizures. The first episode occurred approximately three weeks ago while he was jogging in his neighborhood. According to his wife, who was driving alongside him in their car, he suddenly collapsed to the ground without any warning cry or apparent stumbling. She reports that after he fell, his body stiffened briefly and then his arms and legs 'shook' for approximately 10-15 seconds. She rushed to his side and found him pale and diaphoretic. He regained consciousness within 30-45 seconds of the onset and was immediately alert, asking what happened. He did not recall any preceding aura, lightheadedness, or visual disturbance. There was no tongue biting and no urinary incontinence. He declined ambulance transport and attributed the event to dehydration and heat. The second episode occurred four days ago during football practice. He was demonstrating a drill, running at moderate intensity, when he abruptly collapsed in front of several students and fellow coaches. Witnesses describe a similar pattern: sudden fall, brief stiffening, followed by 'jerking' of the extremities lasting approximately 10-20 seconds, then rapid return to alertness. One of the coaches, a former EMT, noted that Mr. Hawkins appeared ashen and his pulse felt 'weak and irregular' during the event but normalized within a minute. Again, there was no post-ictal confusion, no tongue biting, and no incontinence. He was fully conversant within one minute of the event. He was taken to his primary care physician who noted a normal resting ECG and referred him to the emergency department for neurological evaluation with a provisional diagnosis of new-onset seizures. The patient reports that in retrospect, he has had approximately three to four episodes over the past two months of sudden brief lightheadedness during exertion, each lasting only a few seconds and resolving spontaneously. He also recalls one episode of feeling his heart 'flutter and race' for about 30 seconds during a jog approximately six weeks ago, which resolved on its own. He denies chest pain, sustained palpitations, exertional dyspnea, or lower extremity edema. He is particularly concerned because his father died suddenly at age 48 during a basketball game.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-modulated, reactive to eye opening and closure
background.anterior_rhythms: Low-to-medium amplitude beta activity, symmetric, no focal slowing
background.sleep_features: Brief stage II sleep captured with vertex waves and sleep spindles, normal sleep architecture

artifacts.description: Intermittent low-amplitude muscle artifact, does not obscure underlying cerebral activity
Brief electrode pop artifact at minute 22, resolved with impedance check

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. Symmetric buildup of diffuse slowing as expected. No epileptiform discharges or focal features provoked. Normal post-hyperventilation recovery.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at flash frequencies 1-30 Hz. Symmetric photic driving response at 12-18 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response elicited.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and asleep EEG. No epileptiform discharges, focal slowing, or electrographic seizures captured during this 45-minute routine recording. The normal interictal EEG does not exclude a diagnosis of epilepsy, but clinical correlation is strongly recommended. Given the clinical description of the events, cardiac monitoring may be warranted.
eeg.limitations: Routine 45-minute study; sensitivity of a single routine EEG for detecting interictal epileptiform discharges is approximately 50%. Events were not captured during this recording.
recommended_actions: If clinical suspicion for epilepsy persists, consider prolonged ambulatory EEG or video-EEG monitoring
Recommend cardiac evaluation given exertional triggers and brief nature of events
Consider repeat EEG with sleep deprivation if epilepsy remains in differential

findings: Normal sinus rhythm at 72 bpm
Normal P-wave morphology and axis
Normal PR interval
Narrow QRS complexes
Normal QTc interval
Two isolated premature ventricular complexes noted during the recording
No ST segment or T wave abnormalities
No Brugada pattern
No epsilon waves
No pre-excitation (no delta waves)
ecg.interpretation: Normal 12-lead ECG with normal sinus rhythm at 72 beats per minute. All intervals within normal limits. Two isolated PVCs of right ventricular outflow tract morphology noted. No conduction abnormalities, no repolarization abnormalities, and no evidence of structural heart disease on surface ECG. The normal resting ECG does not exclude a paroxysmal arrhythmia as the cause of the patient's syncopal episodes.
ecg.clinical_correlation: A normal resting ECG has limited sensitivity for detecting paroxysmal arrhythmias. Given the history of exertional syncope and family history of sudden cardiac death, further cardiac evaluation with prolonged monitoring, echocardiography, and possibly exercise stress testing or cardiac MRI is strongly recommended despite the normal resting ECG.

Cardiac_markers.clinical_significance: Normal, no evidence of acute myocardial injury
Normal, does not suggest significant heart failure

Metabolic.clinical_significance: Normal, no evidence of tissue hypoperfusion at time of presentation

Toxicology.clinical_significance: Rules out stimulant or drug-related cause of syncope or arrhythmia

labs.interpretation: Comprehensive laboratory evaluation is entirely unremarkable. No metabolic, electrolyte, hematologic, or toxicological abnormality identified to explain the syncopal episodes. Normal electrolytes rule out hypokalemia, hyperkalemia, hypomagnesemia, and hypocalcemia as proarrhythmic substrates. Normal troponin and BNP do not suggest acute cardiac injury or heart failure, though these values were obtained at baseline (not during an event). Normal glucose and TSH rule out hypoglycemia and thyroid dysfunction. The completely normal laboratory panel, combined with the exertional nature of the episodes and family history of sudden cardiac death, reinforces the need for focused cardiac evaluation.

Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Mildly increased; may represent hypertensive remodeling but asymmetric hypertrophy should be evaluated further to exclude hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
Normal; ratio of septum to posterior wall is 1.3, borderline for asymmetric septal hypertrophy
No resting obstruction; however, provocable obstruction should be evaluated with Valsalva or exercise

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrates mildly increased interventricular septal thickness (1.3 cm) with normal posterior wall thickness, resulting in an IVS/PW ratio of 1.3. Global left ventricular systolic function is preserved (LVEF 58%). No resting LVOT obstruction. These findings are borderline and could represent early hypertrophic cardiomyopathy versus hypertensive heart disease. In the context of exertional syncope and family history of sudden cardiac death at young ages, further evaluation with cardiac MRI is strongly recommended to assess for myocardial fibrosis, confirm the pattern of hypertrophy, and risk-stratify. Provocative testing with Valsalva or exercise echocardiography should be considered to assess for dynamic LVOT obstruction.
abnormal_values_summary: IVS thickness 1.3 cm (mildly increased, borderline asymmetric)

output.rhythm: Predominantly normal sinus rhythm with episodes of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia
findings: Predominant normal sinus rhythm, average rate 68 bpm (range 52-118 bpm)

Three runs of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia (NSVT): 6 beats at 185 bpm (12:42 PM during walking), 8 beats at 210 bpm (15:23 PM during stair climbing), and 12 beats at 198 bpm (08:15 AM during morning jog)
Total of 847 PVCs in 48 hours (1.2% burden), predominantly monomorphic with right ventricular outflow tract morphology
Two brief episodes of ventricular couplets
No sustained ventricular tachycardia captured
No significant bradycardia, pauses, or AV block
Heart rate appropriately increases with documented activity but NSVT runs correlate with periods of increased exertion
output.interpretation: 48-hour Holter monitor reveals multiple runs of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia (up to 12 beats at 198-210 bpm), all occurring during periods of physical exertion. PVC burden is 1.2% with predominantly monomorphic morphology. NSVT in the context of exertional syncope and family history of sudden cardiac death is a significant finding that raises concern for an underlying cardiomyopathy (particularly hypertrophic cardiomyopathy or arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy) or primary electrical disease (catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia). This finding substantially increases the pre-test probability of a malignant arrhythmic substrate.
output.clinical_correlation: The exertion-triggered NSVT is highly concerning in this clinical context. Combined with family history of sudden cardiac death, this constitutes an indication for urgent cardiac MRI and possible electrophysiology study. Exercise stress testing should be performed under monitored conditions to reproduce the arrhythmia and assess for catecholaminergic polymorphic VT.

output.rhythm: Sinus rhythm at baseline progressing to frequent PVCs and non-sustained ventricular tachycardia with exercise

intervals.QTc: 442 ms at rest, 458 ms at peak exercise

findings: Resting ECG: Normal sinus rhythm at 68 bpm, no significant abnormalities
Stage 1 (Bruce protocol): Sinus rate increases to 92 bpm, isolated PVCs emerge
Stage 2: PVCs become increasingly frequent, ventricular bigeminy noted at heart rate 112 bpm
Stage 3: Non-sustained VT of 8 beats at 195 bpm at heart rate 132 bpm, patient reports lightheadedness
Stage 4: Sustained run of polymorphic VT lasting 18 beats at 220 bpm at target heart rate 156 bpm, patient became pre-syncopal, test terminated immediately
Recovery: Arrhythmia terminated spontaneously within 8 seconds of exercise cessation, sinus rhythm restored at 102 bpm
No ischemic ST changes during sinus rhythm
Blood pressure response: appropriate augmentation during sinus rhythm, transient drop to 82/54 during VT run
Dynamic LVOT gradient of 48 mmHg provoked at peak exercise by Doppler
output.interpretation: Exercise stress test reveals reproducible exercise-induced polymorphic ventricular tachycardia with progressive worsening of ventricular ectopy correlating with increasing exercise intensity. The arrhythmia pattern is highly concerning for catecholaminergic polymorphic VT (CPVT) or exercise-triggered VT in the setting of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. A provocable LVOT gradient of 48 mmHg was documented at peak exercise, confirming dynamic obstruction consistent with HCM. The combination of exertional syncope, family history of SCD, dynamic LVOT obstruction, and exercise-induced polymorphic VT places this patient in a high-risk category. This test should NOT have been performed without prior echocardiographic evaluation but was diagnostic.
output.clinical_correlation: The exercise-induced polymorphic VT with dynamic LVOT obstruction confirms hypertrophic cardiomyopathy as the underlying substrate. This is a high-risk patient for sudden cardiac death. Urgent referral to an inherited cardiomyopathy center, genetic counseling, and evaluation for implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) placement are indicated. First-degree family members require cardiac screening.

findings.location: Anteroseptal and anterior walls, maximal at the basal to mid-ventricular level
findings.size: Maximum wall thickness 18 mm at basal anteroseptum

signal_characteristics.T1: Elevated native T1 values in the hypertrophied segments (1120 ms, normal <1050 ms)
signal_characteristics.late_gadolinium_enhancement: Patchy mid-wall late gadolinium enhancement in the anterior insertion points and within the hypertrophied septum, consistent with myocardial fibrosis

findings.mass_effect: Systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve with dynamic LVOT obstruction

findings.type: Dynamic left ventricular outflow tract obstruction

signal_characteristics.cine: Systolic anterior motion of the anterior mitral leaflet contacting the septum in systole, turbulent flow jet in LVOT on cine imaging

findings.mass_effect: LVOT obstruction with resting gradient 12 mmHg, provocable gradient estimated at 45-55 mmHg

additional_observations: Systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve confirmed on cine imaging
Mild mitral regurgitation secondary to SAM
No right ventricular involvement, no fatty infiltration to suggest ARVC
Left atrial mildly dilated (volume index 35 mL/m2)
Extracellular volume fraction elevated in septum at 34% (normal <28%), indicating diffuse interstitial fibrosis
output.impression: Cardiac MRI confirms hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with asymmetric septal hypertrophy (maximum wall thickness 18 mm), dynamic left ventricular outflow tract obstruction with systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve, and significant late gadolinium enhancement indicating myocardial fibrosis. The extent of LGE (approximately 12% of LV mass) is a recognized risk factor for sudden cardiac death and ventricular arrhythmias in HCM. Combined with the clinical history of exertional syncope, family history of sudden death, and documented NSVT, this patient meets criteria for consideration of primary prevention ICD implantation based on current ESC and AHA/ACC HCM risk stratification guidelines.
recommended_actions: Formal HCM SCD risk stratification using ESC HCM Risk-SCD calculator
Genetic testing for sarcomeric mutations (MYH7, MYBPC3, TNNT2, TNNI3)
ICD evaluation given multiple risk factors for SCD
Initiate beta-blocker therapy (e.g., nadolol or propranolol) for symptom management and arrhythmia suppression
Activity restriction: avoid competitive athletics and high-intensity exercise
First-degree family screening with ECG and echocardiography

differential_by_imaging.rationale: Asymmetric septal hypertrophy with max wall thickness 18mm, SAM, dynamic LVOT obstruction, and patchy mid-wall LGE are diagnostic features
The asymmetric pattern, degree of hypertrophy (18 mm), SAM, and LGE pattern are not typical of hypertensive remodeling
No diffuse subendocardial LGE pattern, no difficulty nulling the myocardium, not consistent with infiltrative disease

Tilt_table_test.value: Not elicited after 45 minutes of tilt

output.interpretation: Tilt table test is entirely negative with no vasovagal response elicited even with isoproterenol provocation. Hemodynamic responses to orthostatic stress are appropriate throughout. This effectively rules out vasovagal syncope and orthostatic hypotension as the etiology of the patient's syncopal episodes. The negative tilt table test, combined with the exertional trigger of the patient's events, redirects diagnostic focus toward a cardiac arrhythmic cause.

Genetic_panel.clinical_significance: Pathogenic truncating variant in MYBPC3, the most commonly affected gene in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. This variant has been previously reported in HCM families and is classified as pathogenic per ACMG criteria.
Confirms genetic basis for hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. Cascade genetic screening of first-degree family members is strongly recommended.

output.interpretation: Sarcomeric gene panel identifies a pathogenic truncating variant in MYBPC3 (c.2373dupG), confirming a genetic etiology for this patient's hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. MYBPC3 truncating mutations are the most common cause of familial HCM and have variable expressivity and age-dependent penetrance. This finding confirms the autosomal dominant inheritance pattern and explains the family history of sudden cardiac death (father and paternal uncle likely had undiagnosed HCM). Cascade genetic testing of first-degree relatives (wife for children's carrier status, brother, children) is strongly recommended. Genetic counseling should be provided.

output.query: hypertrophic cardiomyopathy sudden cardiac death risk stratification ICD

results.title: 2024 AHA/ACC Guideline for the Management of Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy
results.key_finding: ICD recommended for HCM patients with prior cardiac arrest, sustained VT. ICD is reasonable for patients with 1 or more major risk factors: family history of SCD, maximum LV wall thickness >=30mm, unexplained syncope, NSVT, abnormal BP response to exercise. LGE >= 15% of LV mass on CMR is an independent risk factor.

results.title: ESC HCM Risk-SCD Calculator validation study
results.key_finding: The ESC HCM Risk-SCD calculator estimates 5-year risk of SCD. Risk factors include: age, maximal wall thickness, LA diameter, LVOT gradient, family history of SCD, NSVT, and unexplained syncope. A 5-year risk >= 6% warrants ICD consideration, >= 4% is an individualized decision.

results.title: Role of cardiac MRI late gadolinium enhancement in HCM risk stratification
results.key_finding: Extensive LGE (>= 15% LV mass) is an independent predictor of SCD events. LGE should be incorporated into risk stratification models alongside traditional risk factors. Even LGE of 5-15% doubles the arrhythmic risk compared to no LGE.

output.summary: Current guidelines support ICD implantation in HCM patients with multiple risk factors for sudden cardiac death. This patient has at least 3 major risk factors: family history of SCD (father age 48, uncle age 42), unexplained syncope (2 episodes), and non-sustained VT on Holter. Additional risk enhancers include LGE on cardiac MRI (12% LV mass) and provocable LVOT obstruction. Using the ESC HCM Risk-SCD calculator with these parameters, the estimated 5-year SCD risk would be approximately 8-10%, clearly exceeding the 6% threshold for ICD recommendation.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cardiac syncope due to hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with exercise-induced ventricular tachycardia and dynamic left ventricular outflow tract obstruction

differential.key_distinguishing: No post-ictal confusion (immediate return to alertness), no tongue biting, no urinary incontinence, normal EEG, exertional trigger not typical for epilepsy. The witnessed 'shaking' represents convulsive syncope from cerebral hypoperfusion, not epileptic seizure.

No identifiable vasovagal trigger, episodes occur during exertion (not standing, emotional stress, or pain), no prodromal nausea/diaphoresis, negative tilt table test. Age and exertional pattern argue against vasovagal mechanism.

CPVT typically presents with exercise-induced bidirectional or polymorphic VT, usually in younger patients, with structurally normal hearts. Cardiac MRI demonstrating septal hypertrophy and LGE distinguishes HCM from CPVT.
ARVC involves RV predominantly with fatty/fibrofatty replacement, epsilon waves on ECG, and RV dilation. Cardiac MRI showed no RV involvement or fatty infiltration.

QTc normal on all recordings (438 ms), no family history suggestive of long QT. Exertional trigger is more consistent with HCM or CPVT than LQTS (which classically triggers with startle or swimming in LQT1/LQT2).

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG on presentation to assess for arrhythmia, conduction disease, or repolarization abnormalities
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm with isolated PVCs; no overt diagnostic findings on resting ECG

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive laboratory studies including CBC, BMP, cardiac markers, magnesium, TSH, and toxicology screen
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All normal, ruling out metabolic and toxicological causes

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for epileptiform activity given referral for possible seizures
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG, no epileptiform abnormalities, supporting cardiac rather than epileptic etiology

optimal_actions.action: Initiate continuous telemetry monitoring during emergency department stay
optimal_actions.expected_finding: May capture intermittent PVCs or short runs of NSVT

optimal_actions.action: Recognize that exertional syncope with family history of sudden cardiac death requires urgent cardiac evaluation, not just neurological workup
Obtain transthoracic echocardiogram to evaluate for structural heart disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Borderline asymmetric septal hypertrophy (IVS 1.3 cm) prompting further evaluation

optimal_actions.action: Order prolonged ambulatory cardiac monitoring (48-72 hour Holter or event monitor)
Order cardiac MRI for tissue characterization and evaluation of hypertrophy pattern
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Asymmetric septal hypertrophy (18 mm), SAM, dynamic LVOT obstruction, and LGE confirming HCM

optimal_actions.action: Refer to cardiology or inherited cardiomyopathy center for risk stratification and ICD evaluation
Restrict patient from competitive athletics and high-intensity exercise immediately
Initiate beta-blocker therapy for symptom control and arrhythmia suppression
Recommend genetic testing and cascade screening of first-degree family members

critical_actions: Recognize that exertional syncope with convulsive features is not necessarily epileptic and requires cardiac evaluation
Identify the family history of sudden cardiac death at young ages as a major red flag for inherited cardiac disease
Do NOT start antiepileptic medications based on the presumptive seizure diagnosis
Order cardiac imaging (echocardiogram and cardiac MRI) to evaluate for structural heart disease
Order prolonged cardiac monitoring to capture paroxysmal arrhythmia
Restrict the patient from competitive athletics and strenuous exercise pending cardiac evaluation
Refer for ICD evaluation given multiple SCD risk factors in HCM
contraindicated_actions: Initiating antiepileptic drugs for presumed new-onset seizures without adequate cardiac evaluation
Discharging the patient to continue coaching and athletic activities without exercise restriction
Performing exercise stress test without prior echocardiographic evaluation in a patient with suspected structural heart disease
Prescribing stimulant medications or vasodilators that could worsen LVOT obstruction
Administering intravenous isoproterenol or dobutamine without arrhythmia monitoring capability
Dismissing the family history of sudden death as unrelated to the patient's presentation
Ordering lumbar puncture (no indication for CNS infection or inflammation)
key_reasoning_points: Convulsive syncope (myoclonic jerks from cerebral hypoperfusion) is commonly mistaken for epileptic seizures and is the primary diagnostic trap in this case
The immediate return to full alertness without post-ictal confusion is the single most important clinical feature distinguishing cardiac syncope from seizure
Exertional triggers for loss of consciousness should always raise suspicion for cardiac arrhythmia or structural heart disease, especially in the context of a family history of sudden cardiac death
A normal resting ECG does NOT exclude dangerous paroxysmal arrhythmias; the initial normal ECG is a red herring that falsely reassures
Family history of sudden cardiac death in two first/second-degree relatives under age 50 is a critical red flag for inherited cardiac conditions (HCM, ARVC, channelopathies)
The borderline septal thickness on echocardiogram (1.3 cm) may be overlooked as hypertensive heart disease but in context warrants cardiac MRI for definitive diagnosis
Late gadolinium enhancement on cardiac MRI provides both diagnostic confirmation and risk stratification information for SCD in HCM
Exercise-induced polymorphic VT in HCM represents a high-risk substrate for sudden cardiac death and is an indication for ICD implantation
This patient must be immediately restricted from competitive athletics per current guidelines for HCM management

metadata.author: NeuroBench generation pipeline
clinical_pearls: Convulsive syncope occurs in up to 12% of syncopal episodes and can involve tonic stiffening and myoclonic jerks lasting 10-15 seconds, closely mimicking epileptic seizures
The absence of post-ictal confusion (>5 minutes) is the most reliable clinical feature distinguishing syncope from seizure
Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is the most common cause of sudden cardiac death in young athletes in the United States
Up to 25% of HCM patients have normal or near-normal resting ECGs, and resting LVOT gradients may be absent in many patients with provocable obstruction
MYBPC3 mutations account for approximately 40-50% of genotype-positive HCM cases and show age-dependent penetrance, explaining why this patient presented at age 55
diagnostic_traps: Normal resting ECG creating false reassurance
Witnessed 'shaking' being misinterpreted as seizure activity
Only borderline septal thickness on echocardiogram that could be attributed to hypertension
Relatively young, fit patient not fitting the stereotype of cardiac syncope
No overt laboratory abnormalities to guide diagnosis
